topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy paper felt cold beneath my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every carefully curated anecdote, every celebrated achievement, was there—but none of it was mine. The narrative flowed smoothly, a tapestry woven with my life, yet the threads felt alien, fabricated. My jaw tightened, a hard knot forming beneath my skin. I reread a passage about my early struggles, the phrasing so perfectly empathetic, so utterly false. A tremor ran through my hand, causing the paper to flutter. I crumpled the sheet slowly, the sound sharp in the quiet room. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, my breath coming in shallow, quick gasps. The air suddenly felt thick, suffocating. I needed distance, needed to scrub this intrusion from my senses.

[story 2]

It was the silence that truly unsettled me. I sat at the mahogany desk, the heavy ink of the biography spread before me. The prose was elegant, almost sickeningly so, detailing triumphs I barely recalled, motivations I hadn't entertained. My eyes scanned the text, lingering on descriptions of my personality that felt like borrowed costumes. A wave of nausea washed over me, a sour churning in my stomach. I pressed the heels of my hands into my eyes, trying to physically block out the words. When I finally lowered them, my gaze was sharp, bordering on hostile. "Who wrote this?" I demanded, my voice tight, strained. The person across the table merely offered a practiced, vacant smile, and that small gesture curdled the feeling in my chest.

[story 3]

I kept turning the pages, a frantic, jerky motion. Each sentence was a tiny, deliberate stab. The sheer audacity of it—someone had taken the scaffolding of my existence and built a façade, complete with convincing emotional resonance. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk. I felt a profound sense of violation, a deep internal recoil. I stood up, moving toward the window, needing the harsh, unfiltered light to burn away the artificial sheen of the document. Outside, the city moved on, indifferent to the carefully constructed lie lying on my desk. I didn't want to look at it again; the sight of it made my throat constrict, making it difficult to draw a full breath.
